8 fact sample(s)
  • Clinical applications of medicinal plant chemicals focus on type 2 diabetes characterized by beta-cell failure, early-stage diabetes, and pre-diabetic states, where abnormalities in insulin secretion lead to hyperglycemia and metabolic dysregulation, according
  • Del Prato S, Leonetti F, Simonson DC, Sheehan P, Matsuda M, and DeFronzo RA studied the effect of sustained physiologic hyperinsulinaemia and hyperglycaemia on insulin secretion and insulin sensitivity in humans in a 1994 article in Diabetologia.
  • When blood glucose concentration exceeds its homeostatic range (hyperglycaemia), the body releases the hormone insulin to promote glucose removal from the blood as a single-mechanism homeostatic control.
  • In type 1 diabetes, hyperglycemia causes a paradoxical secretion of glucagon because the high glucose levels directly impair the activity of pancreatic alpha cells, an effect not accounted for in standard negative feedback models.
  • In type 1 diabetes, pancreatic alpha cells paradoxically secrete glucagon in response to hyperglycemia, a process that occurs even before the destruction of pancreatic beta cells, which normally produce insulin to inhibit glucagon secretion.

  • The pelvic cavity contains the end of the large intestine, rectum, urinary bladder, and internal reproductive organs.

  • Australopithecus africanus skulls exhibit tooth wear patterns similar to modern fruit eaters, suggesting the species consumed mostly soft plant foods like fruits and young leaves, with the possibility of some meat consumption.

dbb8a843 canonical: Clark Spencer Larsen (entity) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Clark Spencer Larsen
embeddings (1): Clark Spencer Larsen
3 fact sample(s)
  • Clark Spencer Larsen of Ohio State University asserts that the diets of early farmers were far less nutritionally diverse than the diets of hunter-gatherers.
  • Clark Spencer Larsen states that early farmers suffered from cavities and periodontal disease due to a diet of domesticated grains, conditions rarely found in hunter-gatherers.
  • Clark Spencer Larsen stated, “you don’t have to be disease free to have children.”
e2c87345 canonical: Rabbit starvation (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Rabbit starvation
embeddings (1): Rabbit starvation
3 fact sample(s)
  • 'Rabbit starvation' is a condition resulting from a diet based primarily on lean protein, characterized by starvation despite high-calorie intake, loss of body protein, protein toxicity, and death after several weeks (Stefansson 1944; Speth and Spielmann 1983)
  • In hunter-gatherer diets at latitudes above 40°, excessive consumption of lean meats from wild game can lead to a syndrome known as 'rabbit starvation' due to exceeding the maximum rate of urea synthesis (MRUS), as noted by Cordain et al. (2000).
  • The Inuit historically consumed the fattiest parts of animals to prevent 'rabbit starvation' and fed the leanest meats, such as muscle, to their dogs, as documented by Fediuk (2002).

  • The earliest well-accepted evidence for hominin meat and marrow consumption dates to approximately 2.6 million years ago at the site of Gona, Ethiopia.
  • Semaw et al. reported on 2.6-million-year-old stone tools and associated bones discovered at the OGS-6 and OGS-7 sites in Gona, Afar, Ethiopia, in a 2003 study published in the Journal of Human Evolution.

  • Dietary energy supply is defined as the average supply of kilocalories available per capita per day for the total population, though it does not indicate what is actually consumed by individuals.
  • Average dietary energy requirement is defined as the amount of food energy in kilocalories per capita per day needed to balance energy expenditure to maintain body size, body composition, and a level of physical activity consistent with long-term good health.
  • Dietary energy density is calculated by dividing total dietary energy by the edible weight of foods and caloric beverages consumed, expressed in kilocalories per gram.
  • Energy intake data is expressed as the amount of energy in kilocalories or kilojoules per 100 grams or 1 kilogram of the daily diet.

  • Emerging technologies impacting open-source governance include AI and machine learning for automating code reviews and vulnerability detection, blockchain for enhancing transparency and trust, and decentralized collaboration tools for secure workflows.
  • Decentralized blockchain technologies offer new models for transparent and tamper-proof governance in open-source projects.
  • Ax Sharma, a security researcher at Sonatype, observes that new protocols like the tea protocol, which use blockchain rewards for developers, are driving users to abuse open source registries to test self-reward mechanisms.

835da7e8 canonical: governance model (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): governance model
embeddings (1): governance model
10 fact sample(s)
  • The Apache Software Foundation governance model emphasizes meritocracy, where contributors earn roles based on their contributions.
  • Openness in organizations is inherently linked to collaboration and the organization of work and allocation of decision rights, which constitutes a governance model.
  • Making governance models explicit in open communities promotes transparency by allowing participants to understand how much time a group takes to consider an issue, the likelihood of contributions making an impact, and who holds decision-making authority.
  • Explicitly defining a governance model helps classify how open organizations are driven by revealing the distribution of power and authority, such as democracy, meritocracy, or benevolent dictatorship.
  • Among the 25 most-starred open source projects on GitHub analyzed by the author, five projects provided detailed governance information, one provided limited information, and nineteen provided no description of their governance model.
42a16d3c canonical: electoral governance model (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): electoral governance model
embeddings (1): electoral governance model
7 fact sample(s)
  • Under the electoral governance model, open source communities establish and document agreed-upon electoral procedures and enact them as a regular method of decision-making.
  • The electoral governance model is more common in larger open source projects where multiple qualified and interested contributors offer to fill the same role.
  • To get started in an open source project using an electoral governance model, a contributor should review the project website for election results and leadership rosters to identify a point of contact, and look for documented processes regarding how the commun
  • Open source projects with a sponsor, such as a foundation, often use electoral governance because the process can make the allocation of sponsor resources more transparent.
  • Electoral governance in open source projects tends to lead to precise documentation of project roles, procedures, and participation guidelines, which helps new contributors maximize their involvement.

10 fact sample(s)
  • The publication of CycloneDX and SPDX v3 SBOM standards, alongside global government regulations, has led to an increase in the number of open source projects publishing Software Bill of Materials (SBOMs) with their components.
  • guacsec/trustify provides a searchable abstraction over CycloneDX and SPDX SBOMs, cross-referencing them against security advisories to identify vulnerabilities.
  • SoftwareDesignLab/SBOM-in-a-Box is a unified platform for SBOM generation using integrated open source tools, conversion between SPDX and CycloneDX formats, VEX generation, quality metrics, and comparison and merging.
  • The 'cyfinoid/aibommaker' project is a client-side web tool that analyzes GitHub repositories for AI/LLM usage and generates AI Bills of Materials (AIBOMs) in CycloneDX 1.7 and SPDX 3.0.1 formats, including detection of hardware, infrastructure, and governance
  • The 'Trusera/ai-bom' project is an AI Bill of Materials generator for agent workflows that scans n8n, LangGraph, and CrewAI workflows to identify AI components and generate SBOM output in CycloneDX and SPDX formats.

aebdda5f canonical: social business models (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings

4 fact sample(s)
  • The concept of sustainable business models is associated with related terms including product service systems (PSS), green business models, social business models, circular business models, and sharing business models.
  • Yunus, Moingeon, and Lehmann-Ortega (2010) published 'Building social business models: lessons from the grameen experience' in the journal Long Range Planning.
  • The combination of economic, environmental, and social business model patterns into a single business model is a necessary but not sufficient condition for achieving a truly sustainable business model.
  • Among 274 articles on sustainable business model patterns, "green", "ecological", and "environmental" business models appear in the titles of 13 articles, while "social business models" appear in the titles of 7 articles.

  • Synergy reflects the joint contribution of parts of a system to the whole that is not driven by common input.
  • Traditional measures of statistical association, known as functional connectivity, cannot disentangle synergy and redundancy, and predominantly reflect redundant interactions.

96eab090 canonical: R-GCN (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings

3 fact sample(s)
  • Neural network-based methods for knowledge graph embeddings employ deep learning to represent triplets, with representative works including SME, ConvKB, and R-GCN (Dai et al. 2020a).
  • R-GCN, introduced by Schlichtkrull et al. in 2018, is an improvement of graph neural networks (GNNs) that represents knowledge graphs by providing relation-specific transformations.
  • The forward propagation of R-GCN is calculated as: h_k^(l+1) = sigma(sum_{r in R} sum_{j in N_k^r} (1/c_{k,r}) W_r^(l) h_j^(l) + W_0^(l) h_k^(l)), where h_k^(l+1) is the hidden state of entity k in the l-th layer, N_k^r denotes a neighbor collection of entity

  • The constrained multi-objective Bayesian optimization algorithm COMBOO balances active learning of the level-set defined on multiple unknowns with multi-objective optimization within the feasible region.
  • LLM4EA (Chen S. et al., 2024) aligns Knowledge Graphs using Large Language Model-generated annotations, employing active learning to reduce annotation space and a label refiner to correct noisy labels.
  • Inaccurate Named Entity Recognition and Relation Extraction prompting results can be corrected through active learning techniques (Wu et al., 2022) or by distilling large Pre-trained Language Models into smaller models for specific tasks (Agrawal et al., 2022)
  • Qian et al. (2020) proposed a method for disambiguating entity names using non-annotated examples, Distant Supervision (DS) to generate pseudo labels, and active learning to address deep learning model data requirements, which involves ranking predictions by m
  • Playful learning engages active learning that is developmentally appropriate and offers a more equitable way of engaging children by increasing access to participation.

  • The duality of language as both a reporting and creating medium is unique to human language and is absent in nonhuman communicative forms.

  • Writers transmit attitudes and emotions through devices such as sentence structure, word choice, punctuation, and the use of formal or informal language.
  • Word choice (diction) serves as an indicator of tone: formal language containing technical terms suggests a formal or scholarly tone, while casual language containing slang or contractions suggests an informal or conversational tone.

  • AM Astobiza investigates whether Large Language Models (LLMs) possess the capability to truly represent meaning and contribute to the development of scientific theories.
  • Georges Rey argued in 1997 that the term 'consciousness' may serve no use in scientific theory and could be dropped from a scientifically-fixed ontology.
  • Critics argue that panpsychism fails the criterion of a scientific theory because it does not explain how a brain produces specific experiences, nor does it suggest new experiments, leaving existing scientific models unchanged.
  • Multiple scientific theories can be logically consistent with the same data set, which complicates the determination of which theory best explains the data.
  • Logical Empiricists, including Rudolf Carnap, attempted to justify scientific theories by translating theoretical claims into observational vocabulary to show they could be derived from observation sentences, logical truths, and set theory.

  • Peircean semiotics posits that meaning is not inscribed in a sign itself but unfolds in the relation among the representamen, the object, and the interpretant.

  • Stevan Harnad defined the 'symbol grounding problem' in his 1990 paper, which discusses how symbols in a formal system acquire meaning.

406e1f1c canonical: RDF-Star (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): RDF-Star

5 fact sample(s)
  • The RDF-Star extension allows single triples to be used as the subject or object part of another triple, providing a feature similar to edge properties.
  • SPARQL is the primary standardized query language for RDF and the Semantic Web, with an extended version called SPARQL-Star used for RDF-Star.
  • RDF-Star improves the formal meta-expressiveness of RDF, but specific cases still require support constructs to be presentable, unlike in Property Graph Models (PGM).
  • In RDF-Star, relations (triples) are identified based on their associated elements (s1, p1, o1, p2, o2), making it impossible to attach different sets of information to equally named relations without causing incorrect connections or relying on support constru
  • G. Abuoda et al. conducted a preliminary analysis of approaches for transforming RDF-star to property graphs in a 2022 arXiv preprint.

8c4f92cb canonical: training-inference mismatch (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): training-inference mismatch
embeddings (1): training-inference mismatch
2 fact sample(s)
  • Large language models experience a training-inference mismatch because the conditioning context during training is always perfect ground-truth tokens, whereas the conditioning context during inference is the model's own outputs, which may contain errors.
  • Exposure bias causes hallucinations because teacher forcing creates a training-inference mismatch where the model is never trained to handle its own errors, causing early mistakes in generation to cascade across subsequent tokens.
d0b89555 canonical: enterprise knowledge (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): enterprise knowledge
embeddings (1): enterprise knowledge
3 fact sample(s)
  • The 'Data Silo Problem' in enterprise AI occurs because standard document processing breaks enterprise knowledge into isolated chunks, causing the loss of critical semantic relationships that span different sections or documents.
  • Large Language Models are effective for general background knowledge, but they perform poorly when applied to specific enterprise knowledge.
  • To effectively ground LLM outputs in enterprise knowledge, a Knowledge Graph must contain knowledge from both database records and enterprise documents, a process Stardog calls 'extending AI safety by extending AI’s data reach.'

8f11cb21 canonical: Thomson Reuters (entity) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Thomson Reuters
embeddings (1): Thomson Reuters
5 fact sample(s)
  • Thomson Reuters uses RAG to improve customer service by helping customer support executives quickly access relevant information from a curated database via a chatty interface.
  • Thomson Reuters' customer service solution uses embeddings to find relevant documents by splitting text into small chunks, embedding each chunk, and storing them in a vector database.
  • In Thomson Reuters' customer service solution, user questions are converted into vector embeddings and queried against a vector database to retrieve relevant documents, which are then used by a seq-to-seq model to generate a response.
  • Thomson Reuters' RAG solution improves accuracy and reduces hallucinations by tailoring answers using retrieved knowledge.
  • Sager CPA was the 2023 winner of the Thomson Reuters Luca Pacioli Advisory Accelerator Award.
3e2419e5 canonical: HybridChunker (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings

  • The HybridChunker component employs a hierarchical chunking strategy that first splits documents at Markdown headers to preserve semantic cohesion, and then applies character-level splitting for sections exceeding size limits, based on the approach by Hearst e
  • The HybridChunker configuration uses a maximum chunk size of 2048 characters with a 200-character overlap and is implemented using LangChain’s RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.
636df773 canonical: Phare benchmark (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Phare benchmark
embeddings (1): Phare benchmark
5 fact sample(s)
  • Instructions emphasizing conciseness, such as 'answer this question briefly,' degraded the factual reliability of the Large Language Models tested in the Phare benchmark.
  • The Phare benchmark, developed by Giskard, indicates that Large Language Models ranking highest in user satisfaction often produce authoritative-sounding responses that contain fabricated information.
  • Giskard is developing the Phare benchmark, which includes modules for Bias & Fairness and Harmfulness, to create comprehensive evaluation frameworks for safer and more reliable AI systems.
  • The Phare benchmark is a project developed by Giskard in partnership with Google DeepMind, the European Union, and Bpifrance.
  • The complete results of the Phare benchmark are available at phare.giskard.ai.

  • The study implements ensemble strategies using a panel of three advanced LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and DeepSeek-V3) to capture the complexity of medical decision-making, with alignment verified against human experts using Macro F1 on 300 cases.
  • DeepSeek-AI published the DeepSeek-V3 technical report in 2024.
  • The Vectara hallucination leaderboard integrates DeepSeek V3, DeepSeek V3.1, DeepSeek V3.2-Exp, and DeepSeek R1 via the Hugging Face inference provider.

  • To fact-check the LLM, the authors use the Cypher query language to return relevant coverage nodes and their descriptions from the knowledge graph, then perform a similarity match between the LLM response and the retrieved knowledge graph information using emb
  • To return other coverages mentioned in the same plan modification document, the authors followed the relationship up to the document node and then out to all other coverage nodes.

  • The proposed knowledge graph expansion pipeline addresses computational efficiency, data quality, evolving knowledge, and adaptive representations in natural language understanding simultaneously.
  • In the proposed knowledge graph expansion workflow, high-confidence predictions are automatically injected into the knowledge graph, while low-confidence predictions are reviewed by domain experts who validate results, insert new relations, provide feedback by

  • Immanuel Kant argued that it is a condition of all thought that it be self-conscious in the sense of being attributed to a thinker, meaning one cannot think without thinking that one is oneself thinking.

  • Immanuel Kant denied that introspection presents humans with any sort of self-as-object, echoing an insight previously held by David Hume.
  • David Hume claimed that causal relations are unintelligible and mysterious because humans do not experience the power, energy, or other qualities that are ordinarily taken to characterize causal relations.
  • David Hume argues that when he examines his own consciousness, he never encounters a 'self' but only stumbles upon particular perceptions such as heat, cold, light, shade, love, hatred, pain, or pleasure.
  • David Hume writes: 'For my part when I enter most intimately into what I call myself, I always stumble on some particular perception or other, of heat or cold, light or shade, love or hatred, pain or pleasure. I never can catch myself at any time without a per
  • David Hume posits that the idea of the self is nothing more than a 'heap or collection of different perceptions' because there is no impression or perception of the owner of these perceptions.

  • John Perry argues that first-personal content is 'self-locating' and therefore enables action, whereas non-first-personal content does not necessarily trigger the same action without additional self-identification.

  • Frank Jackson's version of the knowledge argument utilizes a thought experiment about a scientist named Mary who possesses complete physical knowledge of color vision while living in a black-and-white room, despite having never seen color.
  • C.D. Broad and Frank Jackson argued that conscious states could evolve as by-products of useful brain states, even if those conscious states themselves are inert.
  • Frank Jackson's 'Mary's Room' thought experiment demonstrates that seeing red provides new information that is not entailed by physical facts alone.

  • Adding verbal labels to concrete concepts in neural networks augments the neural assemblies, increasing their robustness and ease of activation.
  • According to standard neurophysiological accounts, mental representations are correlated with the activity of neuronal assemblies, which are ensembles of several thousands of coupled neurons.
  • Neuronal assemblies are difficult to stabilize if the transfer function of individual neurons is strictly monotonic, meaning increasing input leads to increasing output.
  • Hans Flohr (2000) proposes that the specific plasticity of NMDA receptors is a necessary condition for the formation of extended stable neuronal assemblies, which he identifies with conscious states.
  • Henry Stapp argues that mental effort, specifically attention directed toward intentional acts, can extend the lifetime of the neuronal assemblies representing templates for action through quantum Zeno-type effects.

4 fact sample(s)
  • Christof Koch and his research team propose that conscious experience arises whenever a quantum superposition forms, which is the opposite of the Penrose-Hameroff theory that consciousness arises from the collapse of a superposition.
  • Christof Koch states that his team's hypothesis—that consciousness arises when a quantum superposition forms—avoids the possibility of faster-than-light travel implied by the original Penrose-Hameroff theory.
  • The Penrose-Hameroff approach to consciousness addresses several complex issues, including the mind-matter relationship, the unification of physical interactions, the origin of mathematical truth, and brain dynamics across hierarchical levels.
  • After more than three decades, the Penrose-Hameroff approach has inspired research into quantum effects on consciousness across various theoretical and empirical groups, covering areas from microtubuli to synaptic processes and large-scale brain regions.

  • Mary, a hypothetical subject who knows all physical facts about colors, learns a new phenomenal fact upon seeing a red rose for the first time, which suggests that phenomenal facts are non-physical.
  • The knowledge argument, or Mary's Room, is a thought experiment involving a neuroscientist named Mary who has lived in a black-and-white room and knows everything about the brain and color perception, but has never seen color.
  • Frank Jackson's hypothetical scenario of Mary, the super color scientist, illustrates the claim that third-person theory has limits, specifically regarding what can be understood about experiencing red without having had that experience.
  • In a Gettier case adapted from Zagzebski (1996), Mary enters her house and sees a familiar appearance in her husband's chair, leading her to believe her husband is sitting in the living room. However, she has misidentified the man, who is actually her husband'
  • A theological objection to the idea of an alien Incarnation is the requirement for a figure like Mary to be uniquely chosen for each alien species.

  • David Chalmers defines zombies as beings who are identical to humans in every physical respect, including external behavior and internal brain states, but who lack phenomenal consciousness.
  • David Chalmers argues that (1) zombies are perfectly conceivable, (2) if zombies are conceivable, then they are metaphysically possible, and (3) if zombies are metaphysically possible, then physicalism is false.

  • Peter Carruthers authored 'Phenomenal concepts and higher-order experiments' in the book 'Consciousness: essays from a higher-order perspective', published by Oxford University Press in 2005.
  • David Chalmers contributed the chapter 'Phenomenal Concepts and the Explanatory Gap' to the book 'Phenomenal Concepts and Phenomenal Knowledge: New Essays on Consciousness and Physicalism', published by Oxford University Press in 2006.
  • The Phenomenal Concept Strategy (PCS) is often explained by the assertion that phenomenal properties are embedded within phenomenal concepts, which allows these properties to be accessible through introspected experience.
  • The phenomenal concepts strategy distinguishes between two types of concepts: the third-personal physical concept used in neuroscience and the first-personal 'phenomenal concept' which identifies conscious states in a subjectively direct manner.

  • Joseph E. LeDoux and Richard Brown published 'A higher-order theory of emotional consciousness' in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences in 2017, which proposes a higher-order theory regarding emotional consciousness.

  • Global workspace theory is commonly described using a theater metaphor where a light shines on the stage to represent conscious events, while backstage personnel and the audience represent unconscious information processing modules.
  • Global workspace theory uses a theater metaphor to describe consciousness, where conscious thought is like material illuminated on a main stage, and attention acts as a spotlight bringing unconscious activity into conscious awareness.
  • In a discussion with Susan Blackmore in her book 'Conversations on Consciousness', Bernard Baars stated: "From my point of view, the metaphor that is useful for understanding consciousness is the theatre metaphor, which also happens to be quite ancient, going
e7b4fff8 canonical: Weak reductionism (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Weak reductionism
embeddings (1): Weak reductionism
7 fact sample(s)
  • Weak reductionism holds that consciousness is a simple or basic phenomenon that cannot be informatively broken down into simpler nonconscious elements.
  • Weak reductionists argue that phenomenal properties possess an inherent nature that underwrites first-person access, asserting that explanation must stop at this fundamental nature.
  • Nonreductive and dualistic views agree with weak reductionism that phenomenal properties underwrite first-person access, but they reject the idea that these properties are physical.
  • Weak reductionism relies on a claim of parsimony and a commitment to physicalism, despite the lack of evidence that phenomenal properties are physical.
  • Weak reductionists argue that consciousness can be identified with physical properties if the most parsimonious and productive theory supports such an identity.

  • Arthur Eddington remarked: "It is rather 'silly', given that we know nothing from physics of the intrinsic nature of matter, to suppose that its nature is incongruent with mentality and then to wonder where mentality comes from."

dbb44c82 canonical: dual-inheritance theory (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): dual-inheritance theory gene-culture coevolution
embeddings (2): dual-inheritance theory gene-culture coevolution
5 fact sample(s)
  • The field of evolutionary psychology encompasses diverse approaches including human behavioral ecology, memetics, dual-inheritance theory, and Evolutionary Psychology in the narrow sense.
  • Gene-culture coevolution, also known as dual inheritance theory, posits that transmitted cultural information is a significant factor in human culture and behavior that must be included in evolutionary approaches.
  • Dual inheritance theory, developed in the late 1970s and early 1980s, posits that human behavior is the product of two interacting evolutionary processes: genetic evolution and cultural evolution.
  • Dual inheritance theory is considered by some researchers to be a middle-ground perspective between views emphasizing human universals and those emphasizing cultural variation.
  • Dual inheritance theory posits that genes and human culture interact, with genes affecting the development of culture and culture affecting human evolution on a genetic level, similar to the Baldwin effect.

  • Male and female ancestors evolved different traits, such as mating strategies and spatial abilities, despite living in environments with similar general conditions.
  • Irwin Silverman and Marion Eals published 'Sex Differences in Spatial Abilities: Evolutionary Theory and Data' in the 1992 book 'The Adapted Mind: Evolutionary Psychology and the Generation of Culture', edited by Jerome Barkow, Leda Cosmides, and John Tooby.
  • Research measuring brain activity in men and women during tasks where behavioral sex or gender differences are frequently recognized, such as language or spatial skills, has found little difference in brain activity.

  • Myeloid dendritic cells respond to bacterial infections by recognizing lipopolysaccharide (LPS) via cell-surface TLR4 and producing IL-12 and TNF-α.
  • Activated dendritic cells produce IL-12 family cytokines (IL-12, IL-23, and IL-27) which have Th1-cell polarizing capacity, as well as IL-6, IL-10, and TGF-β which have regulatory T cell polarizing effects.
3dc01209 canonical: tissue repair (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): tissue repair
embeddings (1): tissue repair
10 fact sample(s)
  • Monocytes differentiate into macrophages, which are responsible for phagocytosing and clearing cellular debris and dead cells, producing cytokines and chemokines, and promoting tissue repair.
  • In the context of inflammation, T cells produce cytokines that promote tissue repair and regulate the activity of other immune cells.
  • Macrophages and other immune cells produce anti-inflammatory cytokines, such as Interleukin-10 (IL-10), to promote the resolution of inflammation and tissue repair.
  • The resolution of inflammation is critical for tissue repair and the restoration of normal physiological function.
  • Inflammation serves three primary purposes: it establishes an environment for blood cells to move into affected tissue, it activates clotting factors to contain infection, and it promotes tissue repair.
ddf581d5 canonical: CD3 complex (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings

  • T cells, also known as T lymphocytes or CD3 cells, are a type of immune cell that can directly attack virus-infected cells or act as regulators of the immune system.
  • Natural Killer (NK) cells belong to the lymphoid lineage but do not express T cell (TCR/CD3 complex) or B cell (CD19) surface markers, and they are considered the third most important lymphocyte subset.
  • Natural Killer T (NKT) cells are a subset of natural killer cells defined by the co-expression of T cell markers (CD3/TCR complex) and characteristic surface receptors for NK cells (CD56, CD58, and CD161).
  • Peripheral mononuclear cells expressing CDKN2A are unable to survive cryopreservation, requiring the quantification of CDKN2A mRNA levels in peripheral T cells to be performed on freshly isolated CD3+ cells.

  • When an infection occurs, lymphocytes that recognize the infectious agent are arrested in the lymphoid tissue, where they proliferate and differentiate into effector cells to combat the infection.
  • Macrophages and mast cells complete their differentiation in tissues, where they function as effector cells in host defense and initiate inflammation.

  • The relative contributions of energy expenditure (EE), ventilation (VE), and heart rate (HR) to the change in oxygen saturation (ΔSpO2) under hypoxic conditions differ by sex: in women, the effects of EE (28.1% vs 15.8% in men) and VE (4.1% vs 1.7% in men) wer
  • The difference in energy expenditure (EE) between men and women is approximately 5%, and the difference in percentage of energy expenditure (%EE) is approximately 2% at rest and during walking in both normoxic and hypoxic conditions.
  • Net cardiopulmonary cost accounts for a slightly greater proportion of net energy expenditure (EE) in women compared to men in both normoxic (~2% greater) and hypoxic (~3% greater) conditions.
  • As walking speed increases, the proportion of cardiopulmonary work attributable to heart rate (HR) decreases in both men and women under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions.
  • Multiple studies have observed that females exhibit higher oxygen saturation levels compared to males under hypoxic conditions.

  • The study authors observed that females demonstrated poorer ventilatory efficiency in the first half of a high-intensity interval exercise task compared to males.

  • During the luteal phase (Days 18-28), rising progesterone levels may cause premenstrual symptoms including bloating, irritability, fatigue, brain fog, and cravings.
  • The luteal phase is the second half of the menstrual cycle, occurring after ovulation, where the body prepares for a period if pregnancy has not occurred, often resulting in fatigue and PMS symptoms due to hormonal shifts.
  • Over 90% of women report experiencing some premenstrual symptoms, such as bloating, headaches, and moodiness.

  • Field et al. suggest that natural electro-communication stimuli function to avoid the deleterious effects of jamming signals, a role previously attributed solely to the jamming avoidance response.
  • Electric fish mitigate jamming signals by altering their electric organ discharge (EOD) characteristics to shift the frequency content of the jamming signal away from other electrosensory stimuli they need to detect, as established by Heiligenberg (1991).
  • When two electric fish are in close proximity, interference between their electric fields creates a jamming signal that hinders the animal's ability to electrolocate relevant stimuli like prey or object boundaries, as described by Ramcharitar et al. (2005).

  • Integrated circuits are composed of multiple diodes, resistors, transistors, and capacitors.
  • Electronic circuits can feature combinations of resistors, capacitors, inductors, transistors, diodes, and switches to control energy flow and signal processing.

3 fact sample(s)
  • A semiconductor diode, also known as a p-n junction diode, is composed of p-type and n-type semiconductor materials.
  • In a p-n junction diode, the p-type semiconductor is doped with boron to create positive charge holes, while the n-type semiconductor is doped with antimony to add extra negative charge electrons.
  • When p-type and n-type semiconductor blocks are joined to form a p-n junction diode, extra electrons from the n-type combine with holes in the p-type to create a depletion zone that lacks free electrons or holes.

  • Gray whales (Eschrichtius robustus) have been departing the northern Bering Sea and southern Chukchi Sea earlier each year since 2016, although their spring arrival timing in the Pacific Arctic has shown little change.

  • Organisms must cope with unpredictable events, including social disruptions, predators, extreme weather events, and human disturbance.
  • Predator pressure cues indicate reduced access to exposed resources due to fear of capture; they are most reliable when predators are numerous or food resources are concentrated in unprotected areas, but increase error when perceived predators are not a danger
bddf56eb canonical: Armchair epistemologists (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Armchair epistemologists
embeddings (1): Armchair epistemologists
3 fact sample(s)
  • Armchair epistemologists are philosophers who rely on common sense empirical knowledge and a priori information to conduct their inquiries.
  • Cooperative Naturalism is interpreted in two ways: either as the view that general empirical information is important for resolving epistemological issues, which armchair epistemologists can accept, or as the view that detailed information from empirical scien
  • Cooperative Naturalism, when interpreted as the view that detailed information from empirical sciences is important for epistemology, is likely to be rejected by armchair epistemologists.
18e77721 canonical: naturalist (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): naturalist
embeddings (1): naturalist
10 fact sample(s)
  • David Chalmers rejects physicalism but identifies as a naturalist.
  • Naturalists in epistemology tend to focus on questions regarding whether we have knowledge in specific areas, whether we draw correct conclusions from evidence, and whether the processes we use are reliable.
  • The debate between naturalists and non-naturalists can be understood as a disagreement over whether knowledge and justification can be defined using naturalistically acceptable causal and reliabilist terms or if they require naturalistically suspect evaluative
  • Some naturalists argue that a substantive naturalist view must treat epistemic facts as supervening on causal facts rather than logical facts.
  • The Quinean view that epistemology should be abandoned in favor of psychology is not widely accepted by contemporary naturalists in epistemology, as noted by Almeder (1998), BonJour (1994), Foley (1994), and Fumerton (1994).
e80defa4 canonical: principle of perceptual evidence (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): principle of perceptual evidence principle (R)
embeddings (2): principle of perceptual evidence principle (R)
2 fact sample(s)
  • Roderick Chisholm proposed a principle of perceptual evidence (R): Being in the state of seeming to see something red (being appeared to redly) is evidence for the proposition that one really does see something red.
  • The evidential support described in Roderick Chisholm's principle (R) is defeasible, meaning one could possess evidence that they are not really seeing something red despite being appeared to redly.
98ec70a4 canonical: reliable process (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): reliable process reliable process type
embeddings (2): reliable process reliable process type
5 fact sample(s)
  • Epistemic justification is considered a positive property of beliefs because justified beliefs originate from reliable processes that promote the goal of believing what is true rather than what is false.
  • Process reliabilism holds that a belief is justified if it is produced by a reliable process type, defined as a process that produces true beliefs more often than false beliefs.
  • In process reliabilism, vision is considered a reliable process type if the beliefs produced by vision are true more often than they are false across all times and places.
  • Process reliabilism is the view that justified beliefs are beliefs produced by a reliable process type.
  • Process reliabilism asserts that whether a person's belief is justified depends on contingent factors external to the person's mind, specifically whether the belief is produced by a reliable process type.
68d4366a canonical: regress argument (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): regress argument epistemic regress argument
embeddings (2): regress argument epistemic regress argument
7 fact sample(s)
  • The regress argument in epistemology posits that there are two kinds of justified beliefs: basic beliefs, which begin a series of justified beliefs, and non-basic beliefs, which are based on other justified beliefs.
  • The regress argument assumes that the basing relation of justification is linear, meaning one belief is based on one or more other beliefs in an asymmetrical fashion.
  • The regress argument for foundationalism posits that for any justified belief (B1), if it is not basic, it must be justified by another belief (B2), which in turn requires justification, leading to an infinite chain or a loop unless the chain terminates in a b
  • The regress argument concludes that if justified beliefs exist, there must be basic beliefs that do not derive their justification from other beliefs.
  • The regress argument, even if sound, only demonstrates the necessity of doxastic basicality, which dependence coherentism allows for, meaning the argument only effectively defends experiential foundationalism against doxastic coherentism.
23f13209 canonical: Experiential foundationalism (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): Experiential foundationalism EB
embeddings (2): Experiential foundationalism EB
4 fact sample(s)
  • Experiential foundationalists who classify beliefs as basic cannot adopt the compromise position, as they must maintain that a perceptual experience (E) by itself is sufficient to make a belief (H) justified.
  • Experiential foundationalists who prefer Experiential Foundationalism (EB) over Dogmatic Foundationalism (DB) cannot argue that perceptual experiences are a source of justification because the individual has a reason for believing they are, as that reason woul
  • Experiential foundationalists who prefer Experiential Foundationalism (EB) can endorse externalism to argue that perceptual experiences are a source of justification if, and only if, those experiences are of types that are reliably associated with true resulti
  • Experiential foundationalists argue that perception is a source of justification, which necessitates answering the 'J-question' regarding why perception serves as a source of justification.
3ca97df0 canonical: standards for knowledge (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): standards for knowledge
embeddings (1): standards for knowledge
3 fact sample(s)
  • Contextualists in epistemology distinguish between two sets of standards for knowledge, referred to as 'high' and 'low' standards, which determine how liberally or reluctantly knowledge is ascribed in different contexts.
  • When the possibility of being a Brain in a Vat (BIV) becomes salient, an individual's standards of knowledge rise, requiring the individual to be able to eliminate the error possibility of being a BIV to claim knowledge of having hands.
  • According to contextualists, the salience of error-possibilities determines the standards of knowledge, where the presence of a salient error-possibility raises the standards required to ascribe knowledge.
8eb5fe9d canonical: José Medina (entity) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): José Medina
embeddings (1): José Medina
7 fact sample(s)
  • José Medina (2012) argues that people marginalized on racial grounds possess specific epistemic advantages, similar to the arguments made in standpoint epistemology.
  • José Medina argues that credibility deficits are better understood in triadic contexts rather than dyadic ones, as testimony regarding marginalization is often met with denial or discrediting from individuals in dominant social positions.
  • José Medina's 2011 article 'The relevance of credibility excess in a proportional view of epistemic injustice: Differential epistemic authority and the social imaginary,' published in the journal 'Social Epistemology,' discusses credibility excess and epistemi
  • José Medina's 2012 book 'The Epistemology of Resistance: Gender and Racial Oppression, Epistemic Injustice, and the Social Imagination,' published by Oxford University Press, examines the intersections of oppression, epistemic injustice, and social imagination
  • José Medina argues that people marginalized on racial grounds enjoy specific epistemic advantages.

aliases (1): testimonially-based knowledge
embeddings (1): testimonially-based knowledge
6 fact sample(s)
  • Robert Audi (1997) is demanding regarding testimonially-based justification but lenient regarding testimonially-based knowledge because he does not consider justification a requirement for knowledge.
  • Most testimonial liberals include a 'defeater condition' on testimonially-based knowledge, meaning an individual's entitlement to believe testimony is defeasible if contrary information about the proposition or the testimony itself is available.
  • Sanford Goldberg (2008) argues that reliable caretakers act as an external defeater-detection system, which is critical for young children to acquire testimonially-based knowledge.
  • Beliefs can be based on multiple sources simultaneously, such as being partly testimonially-based and partly perceptually-based, or partly inductively-based and partly memorially-based.
  • Sanford Goldberg published 'Testimonially Based Knowledge From False Testimony' in The Philosophical Quarterly in 2001.

  • Solomon Asch's conformity research (1951) demonstrated that 75% of individuals align their choices with group norms, a psychological tendency marketers leverage through testimonials and influencer endorsements.

  • Intellectual virtues can be distinguished and structured by cataloguing epistemic emotions, which include curiosity, fascination, intrigue, hope, trust, distrust, mistrust, surprise, doubt, skepticism, boredom, puzzlement, confusion, wonder, awe, faith, and ep
  • Theorizing intellectual virtues via epistemic emotions provides researchers with a “to do list” because many of the virtues related to these emotions remain unexplored or underexplored.

  • Individuals can increase their income through several strategies: improving job performance and negotiating pay, changing employers (particularly in fields like tech where changing jobs every 3-4 years is effective), seeking positions with equity or bonus stru
  • Inadequate sleep can negatively impact mental health, including outlook, mood, attention span, and job performance.

030a6aa7 canonical: mental performance (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): mental performance

  • Sufficient sleep enhances cognitive abilities, creativity, and overall mental performance.
  • Sleep deprivation symptoms include daytime tiredness, slowed reaction times, trouble focusing, impaired mental or physical performance, and mood changes.
  • Regression analyses confirm that sleep quality plays a predictive role in mental performance when controlling for demographics.
  • Regression analyses in the study of 400 university students confirmed that sleep quality acts as a predictor of mental performance, even when controlling for demographic factors.
1c9c79be canonical: sleep spindles (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): sleep spindles
embeddings (1): sleep spindles
10 fact sample(s)
  • Sleep spindles, which are short neural oscillations that occur during NREM sleep, have been consistently linked to cognitive performance (Bódizs et al., 2014).
  • Research indicates a relationship between sleep spindles and IQ, suggesting that these specific patterns of brain activity during sleep are linked to intellectual capacity (Fang et al., 2017).
  • N2 (Stage 2) sleep is characterized by a drop in heart rate and body temperature, and the presence of sleep spindles, K-complexes, or both on an EEG recording.
  • Sleep spindles are brief, powerful bursts of neuronal firing in the superior temporal gyri, anterior cingulate, insular cortices, and thalamus that induce calcium influx into cortical pyramidal cells.
  • Sleep spindles are believed to be integral to synaptic plasticity and are essential for procedural and declarative memory consolidation.

8314fd1b canonical: Disorders of arousal (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Disorders of arousal
embeddings (1): Disorders of arousal
3 fact sample(s)
  • Disorders of arousal are the most common type of parasomnia, affecting up to 4 percent of the adult population (Ohayon et al., 1999) and up to 17 percent of children (Klackenberg, 1982).
  • Disorders of arousal typically occur during the first 60 to 90 minutes of sleep and involve partial arousal from deep NREM sleep rather than full awakenings.
  • Disorders of arousal manifest in various ways, ranging from barely audible mumbling and disoriented sleepwalking to frantic bouts of shrieking and flailing of limbs, according to Wills and Garcia (2002).
dfb6278f canonical: SIDS (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): SIDS Sudden infant death syndrome
embeddings (2): SIDS Sudden infant death syndrome
6 fact sample(s)
  • Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) is defined as the sudden and unexpected death of infants less than one year old during sleep, and it has no known cause.
  • Siblings of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) victims exhibit aberrant temporal patterning of slow-wave sleep.
  • Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) is related to maternal smoking during and after pregnancy.
  • Maternal smoking, drinking, or drug use during gestation, as well as infant exposure to smoke, are linked to an increased chance of SIDS-related deaths in infants according to research by Schoendorf and Kiely (1992), the AAP (2000), and Iyasu et al. (2002).
  • Infants born with low body weight, born prematurely, or born to mothers under the age of 20 are at higher risk of SIDS according to Malloy and Hoffman (1995) and the AAP (2000).

  • Extremes in sleep duration (less than 4 hours or over 10 hours) coincide with heightened beta-amyloid accumulation, which is a hallmark of Alzheimer’s pathology and a precursor to cognitive decline.
  • Researchers at Johns Hopkins studied 70 adults between the ages of 53 and 91 and found that participants with poor sleep habits had higher quantities of beta-amyloid deposition in their brains, as measured by PET scans.
  • One night of sleep deprivation leads to β-Amyloid accumulation in the human brain.
  • A positive feedback relationship exists between sleep and Alzheimer’s disease, where poor sleep quality and duration induce amyloid-β peptide accumulation, which in turn causes poor sleep quality and sleep deprivation.
  • Shokri-Kojori et al. found that one night of sleep deprivation leads to β-Amyloid accumulation in the human brain.

  • When the attachment system is disrupted by neglect, abuse, or chronic stress, children internalize maladaptive expectations about themselves and others, which John Bowlby termed 'internal working models' that shape relational experiences and self-concept acros
  • Attachment-Based Therapy is based on the theory that initial attachment disturbances, particularly traumatic ones, determine the formation of internal working models that persist into adulthood and influence parenting decisions.

  • To maintain grid stability in a system with very high levels of intermittent generation, Severin Borenstein and Ryan Kellogg propose a combination of four approaches: long-distance power transmission, storage, demand flexibility, and dispatchable generation.

  • Integrating electric vehicles (EVs) into Residential Demand Side Management (RDSM) improves grid stability, minimizes the need for additional generation capacity, and maximizes the utilization of existing resources through load shifting and optimization.
  • The integration of electric vehicles (EVs) into Residential Demand Side Management (RDSM) reduces dependence on centralized infrastructure, minimizes the impact of power outages, and improves overall grid stability.

  • Green infrastructure consists of purpose-built and adaptive design features that incorporate vegetation and permeable surfaces, including urban trees, bioswales, green roofs, living walls, and rain gardens.
  • Green infrastructure differs from green space because it refers to purpose-built urban features that perform ecological functions like reducing erosion and run-off into freshwater systems, even if they are too small to provide significant wildlife habitat.

  • Earth's surface and atmosphere re-emit absorbed solar energy as heat in the form of longwave or infrared radiation.
  • Absorbed solar energy is re-emitted from the Earth's surface as heat in the form of longwave or infrared radiation.
  • The Earth radiates absorbed solar energy back into space as infrared radiation, but greenhouse gases absorb some of this outgoing heat, which leads to warming.
  • Low clouds emit nearly the same amount of infrared radiation as the Earth's surface due to their similar temperature, while high cold clouds emit less energy because of their low temperature, which hinders the Earth's capacity to cool and raises the planet's t
  • Approximately 50% of solar radiation reaching the Earth's atmosphere is absorbed by the air and clouds and radiated as infrared heat.

8 fact sample(s)
  • Debt reduction involves identifying high-interest debts, such as credit card balances, and utilizing payoff plans like the snowball or avalanche method to free up cash flow for savings and investing.
  • The 'avalanche method' of debt management involves prioritizing the repayment of debts with the highest interest rates, such as credit card balances, to save on interest costs over time.
  • Creating a repayment plan, such as the snowball or avalanche methods, can provide hope to individuals in debt.
  • The 'avalanche' method of debt relief saves money on interest in the long run, though progress may feel slower if the highest-interest debts are also the largest.
  • Neither the 'snowball' nor the 'avalanche' debt relief methods involve creditor arrangements, meaning individuals may still face collection calls or fees while following these plans.

  • Homeowners may find itemizing deductions attractive because mortgage interest and property taxes can often exceed the standard deduction amount.
  • Homeowners who work from home may be able to deduct a portion of mortgage or rent, property taxes, utilities, and repair and maintenance expenses.
  • Taxpayers can lower their taxable income by accelerating deductions into the current tax year, such as by prepaying property taxes or making charitable donations before year-end.
  • Itemized deductions allow taxpayers to claim expenses such as charitable donations, mortgage interest, property taxes, state or local income taxes, and certain medical expenses.
  • The debt-to-income ratio calculation includes housing costs (rent, mortgage payments, property taxes), auto loans, student loans, personal or installment loans, minimum monthly payments on credit cards, child support, alimony, and other recurring debts.

d02a7dd9 canonical: traditional finance models (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): traditional finance models
embeddings (1): traditional finance models
6 fact sample(s)
  • Baker and Nofsinger (2010) argue that the traditional finance paradigm places an unrealistic burden on human behavior, despite its appeal at the market level.
  • The growing acceptance of behavioral finance was driven by mounting evidence that standard assumptions in traditional theoretical finance models are unrealistic and require modification.
  • Traditional finance models assume that investors are rational and make choices to maximize their utility, whereas behavioral finance argues that human psychology leads to behaviors that deviate from rational economic theories.
  • Behavioral finance incorporates psychology-based theories to explain market inefficiencies and anomalies that contradict traditional finance models like the efficient market hypothesis and the capital asset pricing model.
  • Traditional finance models assume that investors are rational and make choices to maximize their utility.

42ba18f0 canonical: decision-making skills (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): decision-making skills
embeddings (1): decision-making skills
6 fact sample(s)
  • Financial knowledge and decision-making skills enable individuals to make informed financial decisions by utilizing problem-solving, critical thinking, and an understanding of key financial concepts.
  • Strong financial knowledge and decision-making skills assist individuals in weighing options for financial situations, including deciding when to save or spend, comparing costs before major purchases, and planning for retirement or long-term savings.
  • The development of financial knowledge and decision-making skills typically does not occur until adolescence and young adulthood.
  • Financial knowledge and decision-making skills become increasingly relevant during adolescence and young adulthood, particularly for youth who begin earning money, making independent purchases, managing bank accounts, or borrowing for education.
  • Schools can help youth develop financial knowledge and decision-making skills by providing opportunities to practice financial behaviors, make financial decisions, and reflect on the outcomes and consequences of those decisions.
3ac1198a canonical: insurance company (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings

  • Insurance policy guarantees are based on the claims-paying ability of the issuing insurance company.
  • Cancer patients can identify potential counselors by asking their cancer care team for suggestions, checking with their insurance company for covered providers, or seeking recommendations from other cancer patients through support groups.
811b6781 canonical: genetic mutation (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): genetic mutation gene mutation
embeddings (2): genetic mutation gene mutation
10 fact sample(s)
  • The balance between proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes can be disrupted by gene mutations, and the overexpression of proto-oncogenes may lead to abnormal cell proliferation and tumor formation, according to Pitot (1993).
  • Differentiated cells or premature termination of reprogramming can carry gene mutations and the full genetic heritage of the patient, which may contribute to long-term risk and tumor formation.
  • Factors such as oxidative stress, genetic mutations, infections, and a lack of chaperone proteins can induce endoplasmic reticulum stress, leading to the accumulation and aggregation of proteins.
  • A dominant theory in ageing research for decades has been that ageing results from the accumulation of DNA changes, primarily genetic mutations, which cause cells to lose their properties and lead to tissue and organ breakdown.
  • Not every patient’s tumor carries a genetic mutation that has been identified as cancer-causing or having a potential targeted therapy option, and targeted therapies are not guaranteed to work in all patients.

6a8580c4 canonical: functional space (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): functional space
embeddings (1): functional space
7 fact sample(s)
  • Exposure to extreme events can push a cell to search for a new optimum basin in a new functional space with altered regulatory bounds, which signifies a fundamental shift in the cell’s operational parameters.
  • Cellular reconfiguration causes a cell to transition into a new functional space that is distinct from its original state.
  • A new functional space provides a cell with unique and modified regulatory bounds, which alters both the response time and the nature of the intracellular response.
  • Homeostasis and adaptive homeostasis (allostasis) shape a functional space where phenotypic stability and plasticity define the dynamics and trajectory of a cell’s configuration in response to varying degrees of extracellular changes.
  • The functional space and its minima are uniquely defined for the soma versus the dendrites of a neuron, allowing the functional space in one part to be maintained within a pre-set range while being altered in the other.
fbaa177f canonical: receptor noise (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): receptor noise
embeddings (1): receptor noise

  • Receptor noise studies indicate that slow binding reduces chemotactic efficiency and establishes limits on the fidelity of signaling processes.

b767cfcd canonical: Type 1 allostatic overload (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): Type 1 allostatic overload allostatic overload Type 1
embeddings (2): Type 1 allostatic overload allostatic overload Type 1
10 fact sample(s)
  • Type 1 allostatic overload occurs when energy demand exceeds available energy supply, activating an 'emergency life history stage' that triggers escape responses and emergency coping.
  • Type 1 allostatic overload is characterized by decreased thyroid hormone (T3) levels as the body conserves energy and can resolve once the perturbation passes and resources become available.
  • Allostatic overload Type 1 occurs when an animal is in a negative energy balance, specifically when the sum of all types of allostatic load exceeds resource availability (Eg).
  • Perturbation resistance potential (PRP) reflects an individual's vulnerability to allostatic overload Type 1 if additional perturbations or resource limitations occur.
  • Allostatic overload Type 1 occurs when an animal is in a state of negative energy balance, which is considered an undisputed signal of a threat to Potential Reproductive Potential (PRP).
2ffb62ed canonical: cancer development (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): cancer development
embeddings (1): cancer development
10 fact sample(s)
  • Persistent allostatic states driven by chronic stressors are linked to cancer development.
  • The utility of the allostatic load index in predicting cancer development and prognosis is controversial, with findings varying across populations, cancer types, and assessment methods.
  • Dai, S. et al. (2020) asserted that chronic stress promotes cancer development, as published in Frontiers in Oncology.
  • Chronic alteration of the microenvironment that maintains multicellular organization remodels intracellular processes and induces ageing and cancer development.
  • Mitochondria play a role in cancer development, as discussed by Zong et al. in Molecular Cell in 2016.
49d337c5 canonical: predictive homeostasis (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): predictive homeostasis
embeddings (1): predictive homeostasis
6 fact sample(s)
  • Predictive homeostasis is defined as the normal range of a mediator, such as glucocorticoids, that occurs in response to reasonably predictable events like circadian changes, reproduction, or migration.
  • Reactive homeostasis is defined as the range of a circulating mediator that exists above predictive homeostasis, up to the level where exposure to that mediator becomes damaging (wear and tear).
  • The reactive scope of blood levels of a mediator spans the ranges of predictive homeostasis and reactive homeostasis, extending from the minimum level required to maintain homeostasis up to the point where mediator levels cause harm.
  • When the range of predictive homeostasis is high in a particular life history stage, the extent that hormones may be elevated without inducing homeostatic overload may be smaller.
  • Researchers increasingly distinguish between 'reactive homeostasis' and 'predictive homeostasis' to account for the brain's role in producing anticipatory behaviors (e.g., Romero et al., 2009; Riede et al., 2017; Cardinali, 2018).
aaa152a8 canonical: physiological overload threshold (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): physiological overload threshold
embeddings (1): physiological overload threshold
2 fact sample(s)
  • Potential mechanisms for the physiological overload threshold include critical occupancy of certain receptors, a ratio of occupancy of different receptors, accumulation of products over time, displacement of other steroids from their ligands, or actions of pep
  • The mechanism by which the threshold for physiological overload is effected is unknown and likely varies according to the context of the response, seasons, and sex.
bd485386 canonical: capture-handling stress (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): capture-handling stress
embeddings (1): capture-handling stress
2 fact sample(s)
  • Many birds exhibit reduced responsiveness to capture-handling stress, as measured by glucocorticoid elevation, during the molting period.
  • The reduced responsiveness to capture-handling stress in molting birds has been interpreted as an indication that the inhibitory effect of glucocorticoid hormones on feather development is excessively costly.
71fd0baa canonical: cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis
embeddings (1): cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis
10 fact sample(s)
  • In medicine, the cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis characterizes health as a stable physiological state that an organism must preserve or return to following a perturbation.
  • The cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis characterizes the environment primarily as a source of perturbations that an organism must block or compensate for, which limits the understanding of health in relation to the environment.
  • Bechtel and Bich (2024, 2025) argue that the debate over physiological regulation revolves around the cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis, which is conceived as negative feedback to a setpoint.
  • The authors of the article argue that current accounts connecting health with homeostasis share common limits because they rely, to varying degrees, on a cybernetic perspective of homeostasis based on feedback and setpoints.
  • The traditional cybernetic interpretation of homeostasis defines health as the maintenance of stability and balance, where physiological states are preserved or returned to a setpoint after a perturbation.
65d29534 canonical: immune activity (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): immune activity
embeddings (1): immune activity
3 fact sample(s)
  • Regulatory T cells signal for increased immune activity early in an infection and signal for a decrease in the response as the infection is brought under control.
  • Living organisms must constantly adjust the priority of internal activities (such as digestion, movement, heat production, and immune activities) to avoid conflicts and meet the organism's needs.
  • Lapuente-Santana, Ó. et al. (2024) published 'Multimodal analysis unveils tumor microenvironment heterogeneity linked to immune activity and evasion' in iScience, volume 27, article e110529.
67e9b77d canonical: closed-loop feedback system (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): closed-loop feedback system
embeddings (1): closed-loop feedback system
2 fact sample(s)
  • A closed-loop feedback system consists of four main components: (1) the variable to be controlled, (2) a sensor to monitor the variable, (3) a comparator or central processing unit (transfer function) to compare sensor input against a desired set point to dete
  • In a closed-loop feedback system, effector activity opposes and buffers against changes in the controlled variable.
42f416af canonical: central command (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): central command feedforward regulation
embeddings (2): central command feedforward regulation
5 fact sample(s)
  • Feedforward regulation, also known as central command, is a mechanism of homeostasis where a response is elicited without feedback about the status of the regulated variable, allowing adjustments to be made before changes in the regulated variable have occurre
  • Homeostatic control is organized as a hierarchical, multi-level, multi-goal seeking system consisting of three levels: physiochemical processes (first level), autonomous self-regulation such as the baroreceptor reflex (second level), and central command and co
  • Physiological reflex adjustments during exercise are contributed to by both feedback mechanisms (sensory information from the exercising muscle, known as the exercise pressor reflex) and feedforward mechanisms (central command, such as the anticipation of exer
  • In the Paradigm of Allostatic Orchestration, the brain acts as the organ of central command, orchestrating cross-system operations to support optimal behavior at the level of the whole organism.
  • Chronic stress, or 'assaults on the brain,' are predicted to impact all physiological subsystems regulated by the central command, even when the stressors originate from non-physical sources such as mental, social, or cultural factors.
8e023d2b canonical: Homeostasis as a proportional–integral control system (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): Homeostasis as a proportional–integral control system
embeddings (1): Homeostasis as a proportional–integral control system

  • The 2020 article 'Homeostasis as a proportional–integral control system' by van Veen, Morra, Palanica, and Fossat, published in Npj Digital Medicine (3(1):77), characterizes homeostasis as a proportional–integral control system.
  • L. van Veen, J. Morra, A. Palanica, and Y. Fossat published 'Homeostasis as a proportional–integral control system' in Npj Digital Medicine in 2020.
1d628c4a canonical: overload (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings

embeddings (1): overload

9ae399e3 canonical: energetics-based framework (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): energetics-based framework
embeddings (1): energetics-based framework
3 fact sample(s)
  • In the energetics-based framework by McEwen and Wingfield (2003), Type I allostatic overload occurs when allostatic load exceeds the energetic resources available in the environment, while Type II allostatic overload occurs when allostatic load remains high wi
  • In the energetics-based framework proposed by Bruce McEwen and John Wingfield (2003), allostatic load is calculated as the function: allostatic load = Ee (basal existence) + Ei (routine activity) + Eo (unpredictable perturbations).
  • In the energetics-based framework, Eg is defined as food available in the environment, and Ecr includes Eg while also recognizing endogenous energy reserves of the individual and access to Eg as a result of factors like predation risk and social status.

8e98ec7c canonical: fear appeal (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): fear appeal
embeddings (1): fear appeal
5 fact sample(s)
  • Anti-smoking campaigns utilize the 'fear appeal' strategy, which leverages the affect heuristic to motivate viewers to take action.
  • Hastings, Stead, and Webb (2004) examined the strategic and ethical concerns regarding the use of fear appeals in social marketing.
  • The ethical use of fear appeals in persuasion requires three conditions: do not overuse fear appeals, ensure the threat is credible and supported by evidence, and empower the audience to address the threat.
  • The organization Kids and Cars uses fear appeals in their brochures to provoke the receiver and compel them to open the material.
  • An ethical fear appeal is defined as a communication strategy that supports the credibility of a threat, empowers the audience to address that threat, and is provided free of charge.

  • Amsterdam’s Schiphol Airport reported an 80% reduction in men’s room urine spillage after installing small decals of flies in all urinals, an example of a nudge cited by Richard Thaler.
954284b0 canonical: winner's curse (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): winner's curse
embeddings (1): winner's curse
3 fact sample(s)
  • The 'winner's curse' is a phenomenon where the winning bidder in an auction almost always overpays, meaning the winner loses money. This can be demonstrated by auctioning a jar of coins where the winning bid frequently exceeds the actual value of the coins ins
  • The winner's curse was originally discovered in the field by engineers at an oil company who noticed that the rights they won to drill in specific spots yielded less oil than their geologists had predicted, because the auctions they won were not a random sampl
  • Max Bazerman and William Samuelson demonstrated the winner's curse phenomenon in laboratory settings after it had been identified in the field.

embeddings (1): irrational value assessment

  • Behavioral economists define the tendency to perceive higher-priced products as higher quality as "irrational value assessment."
f4a625a2 canonical: choice set (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): choice set
embeddings (1): choice set
4 fact sample(s)
  • Decisions involving multiple options with varying features, such as consumer goods differing in price and quality, are sensitive to the context created by the choice set, a phenomenon supported by research from Tversky (1972), Huber, Payne, and Puto (1982), an
  • Decisions involving multiple options with varying features, such as consumer goods differing in price and quality, are sensitive to the context created by the choice set.
  • The attraction effect occurs when adding a clearly inferior 'decoy' option to a choice set increases the preference for a similar, but slightly superior 'target' option over other alternatives.
  • The specific context in which a decision is made—including the combination of options in a choice set or the physical environment (e.g., a supermarket shelf, bank, workplace, school, or doctor’s office)—can strongly influence a decision maker's choices.

  • Neuromarketing gained prominence as a research domain starting in the early 2000s, highlighted by Montague et al.'s 2004 study titled 'Neural Correlates of Behavioral Preference for Culturally Familiar Drinks,' also known as the 'Pepsi vs. Coke' experiment.
  • The 2004 'Pepsi vs. Coke' experiment by Montague et al. shifted the focus of decision-making research toward non-rational and emotionally driven aspects, challenging traditional assumptions in psychology and behavioral science.

  • Digital content marketing can increase customer loyalty through visual presentation and brand engagement, as reported by Bui et al. (2023).
  • Li and Aumeboonsuke (2025) determined that gamification features drive brand loyalty, with consumer experience and brand engagement acting as mediating roles.
  • Brand engagement and brand engagement strategies on social media are interrelated phenomena used to construct brand perceptions in the market.

  • The Stimulus–Organism–Response (SOR) model, which emphasizes the 'organism' component, remains a central focus of contemporary consumer behavior research.

  • Rana et al. (2023) applied the Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model to assess how reviewers' ratings influence online purchase motives.

  • Mitochondrial dysfunction increases with age, manifesting as the accumulation of mitochondrial DNA mutations, instability of respiratory chain complexes, proteostasis defects, reduced organelle renewal, and changes in mitochondrial dynamics.

  • M. van den Hurk, G. Kenis, C. Bardy, D.L. van den Hove, F.H. Gage, H.W. Steinbusch, and B.P. Rutten studied the transcriptional and epigenetic mechanisms involved in cellular reprogramming to induced pluripotency.
426d162b canonical: chromatin (concept) — flat · 1 aliases · 1 embeddings
aliases (1): chromatin
embeddings (1): chromatin
7 fact sample(s)
  • The nucleosome, which is the basic unit of chromatin, consists of pairs of core histones H2A, H2B, H3, and H4 and a DNA strand of 146 base pairs.
  • ATP-dependent chromatin remodelers, known as sucrose nonfermenting 2 (SNF2) or switch/SNF (SWI/SNF)-related enzymes, utilize energy released during ATP hydrolysis to rebuild chromatin.
  • Transcription can be modulated by alternate splicing, different promoter or enhancer usage, non-coding RNAs, and epigenetic changes that impact the structure and function of chromatin.
  • Menoni et al. (2017) identified nucleosome remodeling and DNA transcription as two key mechanisms associated with chromatin during base excision repair.
  • Chromatin and metabolism are interconnected, as reviewed by Suganuma and Workman in their 2018 Annual Review of Biochemistry paper.
b3deebbb canonical: naïve pluripotent stem cells (concept) — flat · 2 aliases · 2 embeddings
aliases (2): naïve pluripotent stem cells naïve PSCs
embeddings (2): naïve pluripotent stem cells naïve PSCs
10 fact sample(s)
  • Recent studies have investigated the functional and molecular differences between naïve and primed pluripotent stem cell (PSC) states to understand the relevance of mitochondrial metabolism for pluripotency.
  • Naïve pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) correspond to the pre-implantation stage of embryo development, whereas primed pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) correspond to the post-implantation stage.
  • Naïve pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) exhibit higher oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) activity than primed pluripotent stem cells (PSCs), despite being potentially less developmentally mature.
  • The conversion of primed pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) to naïve PSCs is facilitated by STAT3-mediated activation of mitochondrial respiration and the induction of oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) genes following the down-regulation of LIN28.
  • The increase in oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) observed in naïve pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) does not necessarily result in reduced glycolysis.

  • The proteostasis network (PN) is a macromolecular system that maintains proteome homeostasis by coordinating protein synthesis, folding, disaggregation, and degradation to support organismal health and longevity.
  • Proteostasis network (PN) component aggregation during ageing can elicit aberrant transcriptional procedures, reduced folding capacity, and the accumulation of misfolded species.
  • Chalcone-based derivatives, including nordihydroguaiaretic acid (NDGA), curcumin, resveratrol, quercetin, and isoliquiritigenin, act as dual-targeting drugs that can target both amyloid aggregations and the proteostasis network in vivo.
  • Lipid assemblies act as scaffolding for the construction and function of signaling complexes and play critical roles in the preservation of proteostasis, influencing ageing and neurodegenerative disease development.
  • 4-Phenylbutyric Acid (4-PBA) elevates proteostasis ability in the face of misfolding-induced stress by activating the heat shock response (HSR) and the UPRmt pathway.
